g.data-narration: Welcome! Today we'll explore the Advantages and Disadvantages of Artificial Neural Networks - crucial knowledge for every ML engineer.
Artificial Neural Networks are computing systems inspired by biological brains, featuring interconnected nodes that process information through weighted connections.
Understanding ANN's strengths and limitations is essential for selecting the right ML tool and avoiding costly mistakes in real-world applications.
g.text: Choosing the wrong model wastes resources and produces poor results.
Know the trade-offs before you build!

g.data-narration: ANNs excel at modeling complex, non-linear relationships. Unlike linear models, they can learn intricate patterns through multiple layers and activation functions.
ANNs are robust to noisy data. Since knowledge is distributed across many neurons, damage to a few inputs doesn't cause catastrophic failure.
g.text: Robust to noise and incomplete data

g.data-narration: ANNs process information in parallel - each neuron works independently. This makes them perfect for GPU acceleration and fast computation.
A well-trained ANN doesn't just memorize training data - it learns the underlying pattern and applies it to new, unseen data accurately.
g.text: Learns underlying patterns, not just memorizes

g.data-narration: Deep neural networks automatically learn features from raw data - no manual feature engineering needed. CNNs learn edges, shapes, then objects automatically.
g.text: No manual feature engineering needed - learns directly from raw data

g.data-narration: ANNs are 'black boxes' - it's nearly impossible to explain why they made a specific decision. This is a major hurdle in critical applications like healthcare.
g.text: Difficult to interpret why a decision was made

g.data-narration: Training large ANNs requires immense computational power - high-end GPUs, TPUs, lots of time and energy. Storing millions of parameters also needs significant memory.
g.text: Requires powerful GPUs/TPUs and lots of time
High energy consumption, large storage needs

g.data-narration: ANNs need massive labeled training data to perform well. Collecting and labeling this data is extremely expensive, time-consuming, and often impractical.
g.text: Requires massive amounts of labeled training data

g.data-narration: With many parameters, ANNs can memorize training data including noise. This leads to great training performance but poor generalization to new data.
Designing ANNs is largely trial-and-error. There are no fixed rules for optimal layers, neurons, learning rate, or activation functions - it's an art, not a science.
g.text: No fixed rules - trial and error process
How many layers? Neurons? Learning rate?

g.data-narration: Backpropagation training for large networks is slow. This can hinder rapid prototyping and experimentation in real-world development cycles.
g.text: Backpropagation can be very slow for large networks - hinders rapid prototyping

g.data-narration: Let's review the key advantages and disadvantages. Green shows ANN strengths, red highlights the challenges every ML engineer must consider.
In conclusion, ANNs are incredibly powerful but come with significant trade-offs. Choose wisely based on your problem constraints, data availability, and interpretability requirements.
g.text: ANNs are powerful tools for complex problems but require significant resources
and come with trade-offs in interpretability and data requirements.
A skilled ML engineer chooses the right tool for the job, not the most complex one.

g.data-narration: Key terms to remember: Backpropagation, Overfitting, Transfer Learning, and Interpretability tools like LIME and SHAP. Study well!
